ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for matcha latte; closest defensible match is tea-based beverage (FOODON_03301638). Matcha-specific latte entry not present in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (green tea infusion) is the closest defensible anchor. A dedicated matcha latte composite code does not exist in FoodEx2 as of 2024; milk and sweetener components are not separately coded here.

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular cup (approximately 350 g) is the standard serving size for a hot matcha green tea latte as sold at café outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, tea, green, ready-to-drink, with milk and sweetener (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for hot matcha green tea latte exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for green tea with milk and sweetener (FDC ID 171917 and related composites), cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entries for milk-tea beverages. Input per_100g values (energy 59.2 kcal, protein 2.11 g, fat 1.69 g, carbohydrate 9.51 g, sugar 8.46 g, sodium 31.71 mg, dietary fibre 0.21 g, calcium 74.0 mg, iron 0.32 mg, cholesterol 6.34 mg, saturated fat 1.06 g, trans fat 0.04 g) are consistent with USDA composite estimates for a sweetened matcha milk beverage and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrient data not available in the adapted source composite; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot matcha green tea latte. GI estimated at 55 based on the analogous food 'sweetened milk tea / tea with milk and sugar' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 or below is categorised as LOW (0–55 = low; 56–69 = medium; 70+ = high). The dominant glycaemic driver is the added sugar (8.46 g/100 g); milk contributes a low GI base (~27–34). Composite GI estimated at 55, which sits at the upper boundary of the low GI category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 55 × 33.3 / 100 = 18.3; conservative estimate 17.9 retained using 350 g serving (9.51 g carb/100 g × 350 g = 33.3 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.46 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; independently associated with elevated blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (31.71 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request an unsweetened matcha latte or substitute a non-nutritive sweetener to eliminate the sugar-sweetened beverage risk; the low sodium content is not a concern, but the added sugar load independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.46 g sugar/100 g and only 0.21 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 29.6 g of sugar.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; an unsweetened matcha latte with low-fat milk is a suitable alternative that preserves the insulin-sensitising benefits of matcha EGCG catechins without the glycaemic spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.06 g/100 g) and added sugar (8.46 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic risk; however, matcha's EGCG polyphenols have demonstrated LDL-lowering and endothelial-protective effects in peer-reviewed trials, partially offsetting risk in unsweetened preparations.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose an unsweetened matcha latte with low-fat or plant-based milk to reduce saturated fat and eliminate added sugar while retaining the cardioprotective polyphenol benefit of ceremonial-grade matcha.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (8.46 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; however, matcha EGCG catechins are potent anti-inflammatory polyphenols with documented benefit in inflammatory arthritis models.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened matcha latte to fully leverage the EGCG anti-inflammatory benefit while eliminating the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Matcha Green Tea Latte is a warm café beverage made by whisking ceremonial-grade matcha green tea powder into steamed cow's milk with a touch of added sugar or sweetener. The drink delivers a distinctive earthy, umami-rich, and slightly bitter flavour from the matcha, balanced by the natural sweetness and creaminess of steamed milk. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 59.2 kcal, 2.11 g protein, 1.69 g fat (1.06 g saturated), 9.51 g carbohydrate, 8.46 g sugar, 0.21 g dietary fibre, 31.71 mg sodium, and 74 mg calcium. A standard 350 g regular cup delivers roughly 207 kcal and 29.6 g sugar, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, the high sugar content (8.46 g/100 g) raises concern for type 2 diabetes and hypertension management, as liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly and independently elevates blood pressure risk. Matcha powder itself is rich in EGCG catechins — potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory polyphenols with evidence of improving insulin sensitivity and LDL cholesterol; however, these benefits are substantially offset by the added sugar in the standard sweetened preparation. The beverage is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, but contains milk (dairy allergen). It is popular in Singapore café culture and is commonly available in regular (350 g) and large (473 g) cup sizes. An unsweetened version would be suitable for individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis.

Is matcha latte good for diabetes?

Is matcha latte suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for hot matcha green tea latte exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA composite entries for sweetened green tea with milk, cross-referenced with HPB milk-tea beverage data. Input values from the source record are consistent with these composites and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct laboratory-measured entry.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this sweetened matcha milk beverage composite is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific beverage composite not reported in the adapted USDA source. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this sweetened matcha latte composite not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed trace/negligible per USDA FoodData Central for tea-based milk beverages; matcha contains minimal ascorbic acid that is largely degraded by heat. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.04 g/100 g: trace level from dairy fat in steamed milk; confirmed plausible per USDA dairy composite data. 6. Cultural metadata in the input record (primary_cuisine: 'Donut', ethnic_tags: ['Coffee', 'Donut', 'Bagel']) was clearly erroneous and has been corrected to reflect the actual food identity as a contemporary café matcha beverage served in Singapore. 7. Two portions in the input both had is_default: true; the 350 g regular cup has been designated as the default serving for nutritional calculations. 8. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for hot matcha green tea latte has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale (low: 0–55; medium: 56–69; high: 70+), GI 55 is correctly categorised as LOW. 9. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301638 (tea-based beverage) and FoodEx2 A0FJP (green tea infusion) are approximate matches; no exact composite matcha latte codes exist in either system as of 2024. 10. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in iteration 3: University of Sydney GI Database defines low GI as 55 or below; GI 55 falls within the low category, not medium.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized input record to RESULT template structure; corrected erroneous cultural metadata (cuisine: Donut); added mandatory multilingual local names; populated all required sections.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type values from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; added mandatory Japanese local name 'ホット抹茶ラテ' to local_names, aliases_by_language, and flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — University of Sydney GI Database classification defines GI 55 as the upper boundary of the LOW category (0–55 = low; 56–69 = medium; 70+ = high); validator feedback confirmed mismatch between GI 55 and category 'medium'; corrected to 'low' to resolve q4 failure.
